title: Modern Farmhouse Console Table
summary: A chunky two-tier console with a planked top, 2x4 legs, and mitered corbel-style leg detail. Straightforward joinery, heavy-looking result.
description:
This console gets its farmhouse heft from honest 2x4 legs and a planked top, but the detail that sells it is the little mitered blocks at the tops of the legs — 45° bevels that read like corbels and hide the transition from leg to top. They're the fussiest cuts in an otherwise simple build.

The construction is a pair of trimmed panels (a top and a lower shelf), each made from 1x8s framed with 1x4 breadboard-style trim, joined by a leg assembly. Nothing here needs pocket holes if you don't want them — glue and brad nails hold the trim, and structural screws carry the legs.

The one habit that keeps it clean: cut the trim to the panel you actually glued up, not to the drawing. Two 1x8s laid together rarely measure exactly what the plan predicts, and a breadboard trim that's cut long leaves a gap you'll stare at forever. Measure, then cut.

tools.note: The 45° bevels on the leg blocks want a miter saw you can trust — they show.
For the 2-1/2" structural screws that carry the legs.
Trim and cross supports are glue-and-brad-nail.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs, inner supports, and the mitered corbel blocks.

materials.name: 1x4, 10 ft
materials.note: The two longer front/back trim pieces.

cutList.note: Two per tier — top and lower shelf.
Cut to fit the actual glued-up panel width.
Cut to fit under the top.
Long-point to long-point; both ends cut at 45° bevel, ends NOT parallel.

steps.title: Build the two trimmed panels
steps.body:
Lay two 1x8 boards together and measure the actual width, then cut your side 1x4 trim to that number. Apply glue between the 1x8s (and join them with pocket screws first if you have a jig), then nail the 1x4 trim across the ends.

Build two identical panels — one becomes the top, one the lower shelf.

steps.title: Frame the panels front and back
steps.body: Measure and cut the front and back trim to fit each panel, and attach with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails to the top and side trim. Repeat so both the top and the lower shelf are fully framed. Set both aside.

steps.title: Build the leg assemblies
steps.body: Cut the top inner support 2x4 to match the side-trim length. Attach the legs to it with 2-1/2" screws, then add the mitered corbel blocks with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue — the 45° bevels face out to form the corbel look. Check the assembly for square and adjust for level if needed.

steps.title: Join the legs to the top
steps.body: Set the leg assemblies inside the frame of the tabletop and fasten them, so the legs hang from the top panel.

steps.title: Add the lower shelf
steps.body: Stand the legs-and-top on the lower shelf panel, positioned where you want it, and attach with screws and glue up through the shelf into the legs.

steps.title: Add cross supports, then finish
steps.body:
Finish the base by adding the cross supports with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue to tie the legs together and stiffen the whole thing.

Fill holes, sand smooth, and finish. A pre-stain conditioner is worth it if you're staining pine — it blotches without one.

images.alt: A two-tier farmhouse console table with a planked top and chunky mitered legs
